newCities.history: Vir was effectively uninhabited for centuries — the island served as grazing for Privlaka sheep. After 1976, when the bridge was built and the title-deed laws were relaxed, weekend builders descended in numbers. Over 30,000 houses were built on Vir between 1976 and 2010 — making it Croatia's most densely built-up island. The phenomenon is studied as a case of unregulated coastal development.
facts: Vir has over 30,000 weekend houses — more than any other Croatian island, despite a permanent population of under 3,500
The Vir bridge (1976) ended the island's isolation — and triggered the building boom
Vir's medieval Kaštelina castle (15th century, ruined) was built by Zadar nobles as a defensive coastal outpost
The island's beaches and coves remain attractive despite the development — pebble and sand coves with shallow water
Vir is the most extreme example of Croatian coastal 'apartmanizacija' — uncontrolled holiday-home development

vocab.note: Vir's economy is built on apartmani — rental holiday flats. Croatia's most extreme example of this development model
Vikendica — the Yugoslav-era weekend cottage. Vir's transformation began with thousands of small vikendice in the 1970s–80s
Virski most — the Vir Bridge, opened 1976. The 350 m bridge that transformed the island

newCities.didYouKnow: Vir's transformation since 1976 is documented in Croatian urbanism textbooks as a cautionary tale — the island went from 200 permanent residents and a handful of houses to 30,000 holiday houses in 35 years.

newCities.name: Sveti Lovreč
newCities.intro: Sveti Lovreč is a hilltop village in central Istria, halfway between Poreč and Rovinj. Its 14th-century Venetian walls (still substantially intact), the open-air piazza in front of the parish church, and the surrounding wine country give it the air of a small Renaissance citadel preserved in working order.
newCities.history: Sveti Lovreč was the seat of the Venetian captain (Capitano del Pasenatico) of Pasenatico — the central-Istrian district under direct Venetian rule. The fortification walls were built in the 13th–14th centuries. The village's full Venetian-era layout, with the town gates, the piazza, the captain's residence (now a museum), and the parish church, has been preserved almost intact. Today the village's permanent population is under 800 but the historical fabric is one of the finest in Istria.
facts: Sveti Lovreč's 14th-century Venetian fortification walls are among the most intact medieval defences in Istria
The village was the seat of the Venetian Captain of Pasenatico — central Istria's direct Venetian district
The 11th-century Romanesque parish church preserves 15th-century frescoes in the south chapel
The Open-Air Stage festival in the village square hosts summer concerts in the original Venetian setting
Surrounding hills produce some of Istria's most distinguished Malvazija wines

vocab.note: Named after Sveti Lovreč — Saint Lawrence. The village's parish church is dedicated to him
Pasenatico — the Venetian central-Istrian administrative district. Capital at Sveti Lovreč
Sveti Lovreč's Venetian kapetan ruled over the surrounding villages — a small but coherent district

newCities.didYouKnow: Sveti Lovreč's intact medieval layout has been used by film historians as one of the best surviving examples of a Venetian-era inland town — relevant for understanding how the smaller Venetian territories were administered.

newCities.name: Karlobag
newCities.intro: Karlobag is a small coastal town at the foot of the Velebit mountain, in the Karlobag Channel facing the island of Pag. The town has a long history as a Habsburg-era port whose harbour served as the supply line for the Lika hinterland.
newCities.history: Karlobag was founded as a Habsburg port in 1579 — the new Habsburg-era town replacing a destroyed Frankopan settlement. Its name reflects the Habsburg founders: 'Karlobag' = 'Karl Bag' = 'Karl's Bag' (Bag was the old name). Through the centuries it served as the coastal entry point for the Lika and Bosnian hinterland. The town suffered repeated Ottoman raids, Uskok pirate attacks, and earthquakes. Modern Karlobag is a quiet ferry port.
facts: Karlobag has a strong, regular northern Velebit 'bura' wind — Croatia's most extreme winter wind
The Karlobag–Pag ferry crosses 6 km of channel — the shortest mainland-island ferry in Croatia
The town's name comes from 'Karlo' (Charles, the Habsburg founder) + 'Bag' (the old settlement name)
Karlobag was the historic coastal supply point for the Lika and Bosnian interior — over Velebit by mule
The town hosts the Capuchin monastery — one of the oldest Capuchin foundations in Croatia

vocab.note: Karlobag's bura — strongest on Croatian coast. Winter gusts of 200+ km/h close the highway over Velebit
Velebit — Croatia's longest range, 145 km, separating Lika from the coast. Karlobag is at the foot of the southern Velebit
The Karlobag–Pag trajekt connects the mainland to the eastern side of Pag island — the shortest mainland-island ferry in Croatia

newCities.didYouKnow: The Karlobag bura is so reliably strong that the wind-monitoring station here was used by the Yugoslav army to calibrate jet-engine wind resistance tests in the 1980s — the strongest natural wind in the country.
